g.data-narration: Welcome to C++ Classes - the fundamental building block of Object-Oriented Programming.
OOP shifts focus from procedures to objects, modeling real-world entities with both data and behavior.
A class is a user-defined blueprint combining data members and member functions into one unit.
The key difference: class members are private by default, while struct members are public by default.
Three access specifiers control visibility: private (class only), public (anywhere), protected (derived classes).
Data members store state (attributes), while member functions define behavior - together they form the class.
Objects are instances of classes - created on stack with automatic cleanup, or on heap with manual delete.
Constructors initialize objects - three types: default (no params), parameterized (with params), copy (from another object).
Here's a Rectangle class with all three constructor types - they share the class name but have different parameters.
Destructors release resources when objects are destroyed - same name with tilde prefix, no parameters or overloading.
g.text: Called automatically when: object goes out of scope, delete is used, program ends

g.data-narration: The this pointer is an implicit pointer in member functions, pointing to the current object - useful to resolve name conflicts.
g.text: this->data = data; // this points to current object

g.data-narration: Static members belong to the class, not objects - shared across all instances. Static data needs external definition.
Let's see a complete Student class example with private data and public functions to set and display details.
g.text: public: void setDetails(string n, int r); void display();

g.data-narration: When objects are created, constructors run - when destroyed (out of scope or delete), destructors automatically execute.
Key points: Class is a blueprint; private is default for class; constructors initialize, destructors clean up; static members are shared.
Avoid these mistakes: forgetting semicolon after class, accessing private members, not defining static members externally.
